schools.summary: The first organized opposition — priests, the captain of the temple (the Levite guard-commander, next to the high priest, Robertson, Lightfoot), and the Sadducees, foremost as persecutors and galled at the resurrection preaching (Ellicott).
Calvin/Henry: the captain a Roman officer of the Antonia garrison; the Sadducees set themselves against the apostles most because the question was the resurrection.
Chrysostom: the rulers more shameless since the crucifixion; they sought to make the preaching a public crime.

debates.question: Was the 'captain of the temple' a Levite officer or a Roman officer?

sides.position: The Levite commander of the temple guard
A Roman officer of the Antonia garrison

themes: the priests and the captain of the temple

schools.summary: The rulers 'grieved' (indignant, vexed, unable to bear it, Barnes, Robertson) — the Sadducees at the resurrection doctrine they denied, the priests at the apostles' presumption and the honour given to Jesus.
Henry: miserable are they to whom Christ's kingdom is a grief — they sorrowed at what angels rejoice in; their everlasting grief at an everlasting glory.
The apostles held in custody overnight, no trial possible at evening — the lawful procedure, contrasting with the unlawful night-trial of Christ (Robertson).
Henry: God trains his servants for suffering by degrees — first bonds, afterward blood.

Persecution spreads rather than crushes the faith ('the blood of the martyrs is the seed of the church,' Barnes); the men counted apart from women and children; 5000 the cumulative or aggregate total.
Calvin: '5000' the whole church (Ellicott agrees — the aggregate); the preachers bound yet the word spreading mightily, Christ proving himself alive by the efficacy of his doctrine.

debates.question: Is '5000' the aggregate church total or the new converts that day?

sides.position: The aggregate total of the whole church
The cumulative total of converts (120 + 3000 + more)

schools.summary: The full Sanhedrin — rulers, elders, scribes — assembled to try the apostles; the same council and men who condemned Christ now arraign his apostles.
The arrest providentially gives the apostles access to testify before the highest tribunal (Wesley) — a witness they could not otherwise have made.

Annas (ex-high priest, dominant, still titled in Jewish eyes), Caiaphas (the actual high priest, his son-in-law), John (possibly Johanan ben Zaccai, uncertain), Alexander, and the high-priestly kindred — the same family that condemned Christ, named to show the formidable opposition (Clarke, Ellicott, Robertson).

themes: Annas the high priest, and Caiaphas

schools.summary: The Sanhedrin (in a semicircle, the accused central) demand 'by what power or name' in a tone of suspicion and contempt — implying magic or sorcery, refusing to call it a 'sign' (Ellicott, Clarke).
Calvin: feigned zeal for God's glory, designed to drive the apostles to denial by repeated questioning of a manifest matter; God brings their wiliness to nought.

themes: by what power, or by what name

schools.summary: Peter filled with the Spirit — a sudden inspiration fulfilling Christ's promise (Matthew 10:19-20, Ellicott, Clarke); the man who quailed before a servant girl now stands fearless before the council, his boldness proof of genuine repentance (Barnes).
Calvin: the boldness wholly the Spirit's — 'another manner of man here than he was before'; yet he gives the rulers the honour due their office (Wesley).

themes: Peter, filled with the Holy Ghost

schools.summary: Peter skillfully frames the charge as a 'good deed' (euergesia), pointedly against the council's contemptuous 'this thing' (Ellicott, Robertson); 'made whole' (sozo) carrying a pregnant undertone of salvation.
Calvin: Peter wittily girds the council for sitting as judges to condemn a benefit that deserved praise — arraigning the apostles as malefactors for a good deed.

themes: by what means he is made whole

schools.summary: Peter boldly names the crucified Nazarene as the Christ and charges the Sanhedrin itself with his crucifixion, contrasting their act with God's answer in the resurrection (Robertson, Ellicott); the transformed coward now a fearless confessor (Barnes).

Calvin: the miracle gives occasion to preach the resurrection — the proof Jesus is the Christ; Peter charges the crucifixion home not only to upbraid but to call to repentance and show they have striven against God in vain.

themes: by the name of Jesus Christ of Nazareth
whom ye crucified, whom God raised

schools.summary: Peter cites Psalm 118:22 — the rejected stone become the head of the corner — applied to the council, the 'builders' who rejected the stone God chose (Christ), fulfilling the prophecy they had heard (Ellicott, Robertson; echoed in 1 Peter 2:6-8).

Calvin: the rejection of Christ by the official builders is no argument against him but the fulfillment of Scripture; mere title (Annas, Caiaphas, the Pope) is no guarantee of faithfulness; Christ exalted by God is the believer's comfort.

Salvation in no other name — the exclusivity of Christ against all rival means (Gill); no second name in any land or age (Robertson); the heathen and infants saved, if at all, by the Name even where unknown (Barnes, Ellicott).

Calvin: against the Papists' multiplied ways of salvation, Peter places perfect and whole salvation in Christ alone, excluding all other means; Wesley: a beautiful gradation from bodily healing to soul-salvation.

debates.question: Can the heathen who never hear the Name be saved by it?

sides.position: Yes — saved by the Name even where unknown, as the symbol of divine saving energy
Salvation is in Christ alone, excluding all other means (the strict exclusivity)

themes: neither is there salvation in any other

schools.summary: 'Unlearned and ignorant' = laymen without rabbinic training (not 'ignorant,' Robertson, Ellicott; the source of 'idiot'); their boldness astonished the council; Barnes guards against a charter for an unlearned ministry — Christ taught them and chose the learned Paul.
Calvin: the council, knowing them unlearned, acknowledge something more than human in their boldness, yet break out into tyranny against an evil conscience.

themes: the boldness of Peter and John

schools.summary: The healed man standing whole is living, irrefutable proof; the council could say nothing against it (Barnes, Robertson); the undeniable miracle proves the doctrine (Clarke) — the helplessness of unbelief before God's work.

themes: the man which was healed standing with them

schools.summary: The council, unable to answer the miracle, put the apostles out and confer privately — turning from judicial inquiry to political calculation (Henry); Luke records the closed session, exposing the rulers' bad faith and helplessness.

The council confesses the miracle ('manifest to all, we cannot deny it') yet debates expediency, not justice — abandoning their office as a court (Ellicott); the forced admission the involuntary testimony of enemies to the truth (Clarke, Robertson).

themes: what shall we do to these men
a notable miracle, manifest to all

schools.summary: The council resorts to threats ('threaten them with threats,' a Hebraism indicating an Aramaic source, Ellicott), commanding silence 'in this name' (a contemptuous slur, Robertson); the motive to stop the gospel lest all Jerusalem be converted (Clarke).
Calvin: authority abused rages most boldly where reverence for God fails; the believer may lawfully refuse to obey commands that oppose God.

themes: speak in this name no more

schools.summary: A total prohibition — not to speak privately or teach publicly, the very name not to pass their lips (Ellicott, Wesley); the council's desperate resort to command, recognizing the power lay in the name they could not bear.
Clarke: men bear any doctrine but the gospel of Christ crucified — human nature at enmity with what comes from God.

themes: nor teach in the name of Jesus

schools.summary: The great principle of liberty of conscience — one of the earliest, boldest appeals for the right of private judgment against dominion over the soul (Barnes); bounded by Ellicott: the right of a conscience recognizing divine authority to resist a human authority opposing it, the onus on the one claiming the higher authority.

Calvin: obedience to evil pastors who hold lawful office is contrary to God; all traditions examined by the Word; authorities obeyed only so far as they rob not God of his right; Wesley compares Socrates.

themes: whether it be right in the sight of God
to hearken unto you more than unto God

schools.summary: 'We are not able not to speak' (the double negative, Robertson) — the necessity of commanded witnesses (Acts 1:8, Ellicott), the courage of martyrs; Barnes draws the long lesson of religious liberty from the spread of the Bible.

Calvin: the necessity is specifically the gospel — to silence Christ's witnesses is to abolish the grace of God and the salvation of men; woe to the sluggishness that yields to so wicked a prohibition.

themes: the things which we have seen and heard

schools.summary: The council, unable to punish for fear of the people who glorified God (Luke's characteristic note, Ellicott), let them go — their authority dependent on the multitude (Barnes); the people's praise mocked the Sanhedrin's helplessness (Robertson).
Calvin: the council's restraint God's secret providence binding the wicked with invisible bonds, preserving his children; yet the victory is 'under the cross' — threatened and charged again.

themes: finding nothing how to punish them

schools.summary: The man's age (over forty, lame from birth) magnifies the miracle — beyond natural cure, unmistakably divine, the testimony the more credible (Gill, Barnes); Luke's characteristic precision in noting duration (Ellicott).

Chrysostom: the apostles tell the tale not for their own glory but to display Christ's grace, making the hearers courageous; the church flees to God, the invincible Ally, with earnest, ordered prayer.
The released apostles go straight to 'their own company' to report — the unity and mutual encouragement of the persecuted church, the occasion of the united prayer (vv.24-30).

themes: they went to their own company
reported all the chief priests said

schools.summary: The church's united prayer addresses God as 'Despotes,' the absolute Sovereign Master (Robertson, Ellicott) and Creator of all — the earliest recorded prayer of the church, beginning with God's sovereignty (Ellicott); 'with one accord' (Luke's phrase) marks perfect unity.
Calvin: they appeal to the Creator's almighty power as the ground of confidence against the threats of men — all things in his hand, every danger preventable.

themes: lifted up their voice with one accord
which hast made heaven and earth

schools.summary: The church cites Psalm 2:1, ascribed to David by the Spirit (a testimony to inspiration, Barnes); the 'raging' the tumultuous opposition of the wicked, the 'vain things' their futile plans against God's purpose; the opposition foretold and foretold to be vain.
Calvin: the Psalm's double reference — David's hard-won kingdom prefigured Christ's, against which the nations rage in vain; the Spirit spoke of this very hour.

themes: by the mouth of thy servant David

schools.summary: Psalm 2 continued — the kings and rulers (Herod, Pilate) gathered against the Lord and his Christ (Gill); opposition to Christ is opposition to God, for Christ is equal with the Father, appointed and dear to him (Barnes).
Calvin: all who refuse to submit to Christ make war against God, who reigns in his Son alone; the rebels find God their open enemy unless they embrace Christ and his gospel.

themes: the kings of the earth stood up
against the Lord and against his Christ
opposition to Christ is opposition to God

schools.summary: The four parties of Psalm 2 fulfilled in Herod and Pilate (the kings), the Gentiles (Rome), and Israel — an undesigned coincidence with Herod's part (Luke 23:12, Robertson, Ellicott); 'child' better 'Servant' (Barnes), matching David in v.25.
Calvin: the prophecy proved true by the event confirms faith; though the enemies seemed to triumph, their rage was vain, for they could do no more than God appointed.

Calvin: God governs all things by his secret counsel, bringing to pass what he determined even through the wicked (who act freely from malice); the verse refutes those who admit only foreknowledge — God 'appointed before' what was done.

The providence/free-agency problem stated without philosophical solution — both facts held ('Man proposes, God disposes,' Ellicott); the wicked acted freely yet fulfilled God's counsel (Gill, Robertson).

debates.question: How is divine sovereignty held with human free agency in the crucifixion?

sides.position: God governs all by his secret counsel, determining the act through the wicked
Both facts held without a philosophical solution

themes: whatsoever thy hand and counsel determined
what men meant for evil, God for good

schools.summary: The church prays not for safety or vengeance but for boldness to speak — resolved to persevere, asking only not to be deterred (Barnes); they commit their danger to God ('behold their threatenings,' Clarke).
Calvin: they seek not their own quiet but liberty to preach the gospel everywhere; Chrysostom marvels at the largeness of mind — not 'Crush them' but 'Grant us boldness.'

themes: grant boldness to speak thy word

schools.summary: The church asks for healing and signs by the name of Jesus — to confirm and spread the gospel, not for their own safety (Barnes); the assonance of 'healing' (iasis) and 'Jesus' (Ellicott).
Calvin: they set light by all else for the glory of God, desiring above all that his power be declared by miracles confirming the word.

themes: stretching forth thine hand to heal
signs and wonders by the name of Jesus

schools.summary: The prayer answered at once — the place shaken (an earthquake, a token of God's presence, Barnes, Robertson), all filled afresh with the Spirit and speaking boldly; a fresh communication beyond Pentecost, the Christian needing frequent influences from on high (Clarke).
Calvin: the twofold answer — the visible sign of God's presence (the shaking) and the substance (greater boldness), the fruit on which we should rather dwell.
Henry: the believers of one heart and soul — the blessed fruit of Christ's command to love and his prayer that they be one; the many made intimately one in Christ.

Calvin: 'all things common' was voluntary, springing from love, not a binding law — refuting the Anabaptists; not all sold all (cf. Ananias 5:4, Barnabas v.36); the unity of heart the root, the liberality the fruit.

debates.question: Does 'all things common' mandate communism or abolish private property?

sides.position: No — voluntary, springing from love, not a binding law (against the Anabaptists)

themes: of one heart and of one soul

schools.summary: The apostles witness the resurrection with 'great power' (efficacy and courage in preaching, Barnes, Henry) — the heart of the gospel and the point of the Sadducees' attack (Robertson); 'great grace' either God's favour or the people's (left open, Ellicott).
Calvin: the resurrection alone named as the fulfilling of the gospel; 'great grace' connected to liberality — 'beloved, because they were beneficial,' the bounty opening the people's hearts (Barnes).

themes: great power gave the apostles witness
the resurrection of the Lord Jesus

schools.summary: None lacked, for possessors sold lands and houses for the poor — but 'as many' not pressed (not all sold all), no obligation (cf. Ananias 5:4, Barnes); the selling continuous, as need arose (Robertson); perhaps also faith in the coming desolation (Ellicott).
Calvin: they sold only so much as need required, distributed wisely as each had need, not equally divided; no command but the fruit of grace and love (Wesley).

themes: neither was there any that lacked

schools.summary: The proceeds laid at the apostles' feet (the Eastern gesture of entrusting an offering, Ellicott), showing cordial detachment (Clarke); distribution to each according to need; the office soon led to the seven (Acts 6:1, Barnes).
Calvin: distribution by need, not equality — a discreet supply of want, the principle of Christian charity governed by wisdom.

themes: laid them at the apostles' feet

schools.summary: Barnabas (Joseph the Levite of Cyprus) surnamed 'son of consolation/exhortation' — the name marking his gift of encouragement and persuasive speech, the work of the Paraclete (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott); singled out as the eminent example, the foil to Ananias.
Chrysostom: Luke mentions Barnabas's virtuous deed first to set the contrast with Ananias's worst-described sin.

Barnabas sold his land (a Levite might own land by purchase/inheritance, as Jeremiah, Ellicott) and laid the money at the apostles' feet — the named illustration of v.34, arguing not all sold all but were ready to (Robertson); the foil to Ananias.
Calvin: perhaps named because he sold his only possession, surpassing the rest; his self-chosen poverty later led him, like Paul, to work for his living (Ellicott).

themes: laid it at the apostles' feet
the true giver vs the false
